speaker: interviewer
text: The date is January 30, 1997; survivor, Kurt Lewin; interviewer, Naomi Rappaport. The city is New York. The state, New York. The country, USA; language, English. My name is Naomi Rappaport, N-A-O-M-I, R-A-P-P-A-P-O-R-T. And today is January 30, 1997. I am conducting an interview with Mr. Kurt Lewin in New York, New York, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mr. Lewin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Mrs. Rappaport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you very much for welcoming us to your home and sharing your testimony with the Shoah Foundation. We would like to start with your name. Could you give us your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Kurt, K-U-R-T, Lewin, L-E-W-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was the same, but my middle name was Isaac, or in Hebrew, Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell both Isaac and Yitzhak?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be I-S-A-A-C, or Y-I-T, like in Thomas, Z-H-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I lived under assumed names as a Ukrainian, after my escape from the ghetto, as Roman or Roman Mytka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R, like in Robert, O-M, like in Mary, A-N; Mytka-- M, like in Mary, Y-T, like in Thomas, K-A. After moving on to Lublin-- to the liberated part of Poland, by the red army-- because of anti-semitism and killing of Jews, I assumed a Polish name. I kept the same first name, which was Roman. But I used the name Matkowski, which is M-A-T, like in Thomas, K-O-W-S-K-I. And under this name, I served at the end of the Second World War, in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other names? No. Nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on February 28, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 71 years old. In another few weeks, I will be 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born? In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Katowice, in Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Katowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-T, like in Thomas, O-W-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Silesia was what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a complicated question, because Silesia is on the borderline of Poland and Germany. At certain times, it was Germany. At other times, it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born, what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Did your family always live in Katowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally, the family started in Amsterdam, according to my knowledge. I come from a rabbinical family, and most of my ancestors were rabbis. And they moved from place to place, according to the rabbinical chair which they occupied at a given time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was the rabbi in Katowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Ezekiel Lewin. After taking his PhD at the Jagiellonian University in Krakow and competing his rabbinical studies, was ordained as an orthodox rabbi. And his first rabbinical chair which he occupied was Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Katowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little-- I was a child. I left Katowice when I was about three years old. So my memories are rather vague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you move to-- where did you move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I moved with my parents to Lwow. I pronounce the name of the city in Polish. It would be, probably, useful if I will indicate that the city in Polish is Lwow. In German, it is [? Numberg. ?] In Russian, it is [? Lviv. ?] In Ukrainian, it is Lviw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Lviw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lviw would be spelled L-V, like in Victor, I-W. That's the way it's spelled and pronounced, but in Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the move at all, when you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall? How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have very few recollections from Katowice as a child. But I distinctly remember my father being greeted by the community in Katowice. And quite a number of people came to the railroad station to say goodbye to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Lwow on a street called [INAUDIBLE], 3rd of May, number 7.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the 3rd of May celebrating?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is the day where the Polish [INAUDIBLE], an equivalent of Congress, has accepted, a-- formally, a constitution-- a new constitution which is called the Constitution of 3rd of May. And it is modeled, somewhat, on the American Constitution, or related.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know the year, the 3rd of May--

speaker: interviewee
text: You got me, but I would assume it's 1796 or '95.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of house did you live in, what kind of dwelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a apartment house which was built, probably, at the turn of the century-- as Lwow was an administrative center of Austro-Hungary, of southern Poland. It was a house quite similar to the ones you find in Vienna, or equivalent of Park Avenue, in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father had his office there, his library. And there were living quarters. To my recollection, there were about seven rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my younger brother was born there, at the time when we lived in Lwow, and shortly after we arrived. And my youngest brother, who was born a few years later. So this was the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have help in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had, first of all, a housekeeper and a nanny for the children. And in addition, every morning a secretary of my father-- who was provided by the Jewish community-- used to come and be there during office hours, equivalent of 9:00

speaker: interviewer
text: The synagogue, which your father was a rabbi-- what kind of synagogue was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question the question-- because the tendency would be to relate it to the Jewish theological structure here, in the United States, which is orthodox, conservative, and reformed. It was neither. But after the Haskalah, there were movements in the Jewish community-- in Germany and in Austria, which eventually spilled into Poland-- to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adapt the form of worship to different times-- namely, to the emancipation of Jews and no longer existence in ghettos. You had, in Vienna, this movement-- in Leipzig, in Frankfurt. And they called it enlightened movement or progressive movement. In fact, these were orthodox congregations, but with a more formal form of worship. And they were worshiping in, what they referred to, temples. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The community in Katowice was German-oriented. And therefore, it was a reflection of the same movement in Germany. The community in Lwow was more Vienna-oriented, and reflected the same movement in Vienna. My father was an orthodox rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship-- if you know-- or did you have anything to do with the Hasidic movement, at all, in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I-- I didn't have any direct relationship with the Hasidic movement, but I'm aware of what it was. Hasidic movements stem from the days of Baal Shem Tov. They were perverted-- and I use my word specifically and not by accident-- by subsequent evolution of Jewish sects which were headed by dynastic rabbinical families, like [INAUDIBLE], Belz, Sudmore. And there were [? Kumarna, ?] [INAUDIBLE], many, many.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were they also in Lwow as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were everywhere. But they were basically a minority.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you relate to them, at all? You and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: The relationship was friendly, and the relationship was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somewhat delicate because my family was a family of [INAUDIBLE]. I don't wish to go into a lengthy discussion of what [INAUDIBLE] means. And this was a predominant trend in the Jewish community in Poland and in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your father dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. My father wore what was referred to in Austria, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a suit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with pants, but a three-quarter jacket, double-breasted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a rabbinical outfit?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is in-- not a rabbinical outfit because rabbis, you see, in orthodox community wore a [INAUDIBLE]. This was a Western, but it was three quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear a hat at all times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always, yes, and the yarmulke. Always-- a yarmulke at home, but a hat on the street. No question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, did she have her head covered?

speaker: interviewee
text: She would wear a hat. My mother used to dress in the Western fashion, as most other women did. Certainly not a sheitel, which was considered a barbaric sort of a custom in the community at large.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home, did she have her head covered, or did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't. And what about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wore normal clothing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt, as a child, quite oppressed by the fact that I was in the public eye. My father was a very prominent man. So I was known, and I was observed. And I resented it. I tried not to embarrass my father, but I wouldn't necessarily cover my head unless I was in some public function also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke, in Katowice, German-- because this was a primarily German Jewish community. And my first language, therefore, was German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lwow, I spoke Polish. Most Jews in southern Poland and places-- Krakow, Tarnow, Rzeszow, Lwow-- used Polish. And the community spoke a beautiful Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of ritual, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] did you attend services every day, every week? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended services, usually, on Saturdays and on holidays. My father attended services every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what holidays were celebrated in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The usual cycle of Jewish holidays, which you'll find in every traditional Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was, for you, the most memorable one-- holiday as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, I found the-- [HEBREW]-- I use a Hebrew expression, which means the new years. Yom Kippur [INAUDIBLE], rather oppressive. And I liked, very much, the [NON-ENGLISH], which means the three pilgrimage holidays-- Passover, Shavu'ot, Sukkot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a traditional Saturday at home, which I loved very much. As my father was a prominent man and was under continuous pressure of meetings and duties, so it's only on Friday and Saturday that we have-- had him to ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Friday night like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the usual. My father used to come from the synagogue. And as he was orthodox, he used to put, actually, a silk [INAUDIBLE] and a girdle [INAUDIBLE]. And used to pray and sing the welcoming hymns, which are traditional. And we had the kiddush.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I had little interest in it. And not only that, as I have pointed out, I had a slight resentment because the pressure's on me. And when I do other things, which I experienced in the outside world, so I didn't know where I am. Consequently, I kept relatively neutral-- friendly and neutral. I liked the Shabbat because of the atmosphere of the house, which was a traditional, cultivated Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cooked the Shabbat meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: The housekeeper. My mother was a president of many women's organizations, and so forth. And the pressures on her were equally great as on my father, because the wife of the rabbi had quite a number of social and-- duties.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was the deal-- what did the meal consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The meal consisted, usually, what you would find in every Jewish home in those days-- namely, of gefilte fish, and soup, and meat, and some dessert, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father bring anyone home as a guest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was always a guest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of guests was he bringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one guest, was a very strange man because he was always, on Fridays, invited to the rabbi's house, traditionally. So as there were two chief rabbis who shared the rabbinical chairs, one Friday night he spent with one family, and one Friday night was another one. His name was Senator Bodek Jakub Bodek. And he was a senator. He was elected senator, representing the Jewish community in the Polish [INAUDIBLE], or senate, which is the equivalent of the Congress. He was the director of the railroad district for the Austro-Hungarian government. He was engaged to a young lady who died very shortly before the wedding. And the man dedicated his life to charity. And all his life, he remained single, and just simply addressed himself to servicing the poor, the people in need. And therefore, traditionally, the rabbis greeted him on Friday. And he was always a guest, either at my father's home or the other rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we leave the food issue, who did the shopping? Your mother or the housekeeper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the housekeeper. My mother was too busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The housekeeper. And who kept track of the kashrut? The housekeeper, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. [LAUGHS] That is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you considered--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I have to stress that my father was an orthodox rabbi. And consequently, all the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] restrictions and way of life which you would find in any Jewish orthodox home were present in my home. No question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which were, for you, the most difficult restrictions?

speaker: interviewee
text: The restrictions of kashrut and things like that didn't bother me too much. I just had to behave in-- in a way, in a fashion that people wouldn't come to my father saying that I'm behaving like a Gentile child, and get into fights and things of that sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family considered, economically?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Middle class? Wealthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Position of the rabbi in the Old World was entirely different than it is here in the United States. These people were law-givers. They administered law, because Jews refrain from going to courts. They were spiritual leaders of the community, and very highly-regarded and respected. Rarely, if ever, they were paid a very generous salary. So they-- their lifestyle was relatively modest. My impression is, is my father was a chief rabbi of a very large, very wealthy community. He was paid-- looking at it from my perspective today-- adequately. We were not in a need. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But my impression is that we could not afford too many luxuries. However, my mother was a rather wealthy, because her father was an estate owner-- very large estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: The street on which you lived-- was that a residential area or a business--

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily a residential.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went first to public school in Lwow. And the public school system, which was left over by Austro-Hungary, was excellent. And the school's name was Adam Mickiewicz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very famous Polish writer and poet-- more or less a contemporary of Byron and Pushkin. Was a very fine school. There I finished six grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were there a lot of Jewish children in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The composition of the city has, in a way, determined the composition of children in the school. One third was Jewish. One third was Polish, and one third was Lithuanian or Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it coed? Were there girls--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the classes as well? No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about the staff, the teachers? Were they all Polish or-- All Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: --divided that way too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish primarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any Jewish teachers at all, in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Jews were teaching in some schools, but it was not common to see many Jewish teachers in the public school system.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with the-- your-- your-- your fellow students?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a curiosity because, first of all, my father was a leader of men and leader of the community and well-known in Jewish and non-Jewish circles. Secondly, because of the position of my father, I did not attend school on Saturdays which, in a way, was a handicap. Because all through my schooling, I missed Saturdays. I was not a very good student. And I was always observed. So my memories are mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the teachers and other students. Because being my father's son, I was expected to perform in a competent and remarkable way, which I wasn't able to do. End of story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your social relationship with the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: After religion was introduced to the curriculum in the second grade, the Roman Catholics were instructed by a Roman Catholic priest, the Lithuanian by a Greek Catholic priest, and the Jews by a teacher of religion. So the minute religion was introduced, you had first signs of anti-semitism and friction between the kids. The Poles antagonistic toward the Lithuanians, who were Greek Catholic, Byzantine Catholics, and both against Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that manifest itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fights, very simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- do you recall any personal experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many. But it's a long story and, frankly, not interesting, because this was a way of life. A Jew had to defend himself, or be beaten up. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I used to beat up others. I was rather a scrappy little boy, and knew how to defend myself. And occasionally, I ran into difficulties. But I was exposed to anti-semitism and direct attacks, since the age of, probably, eight. It's way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any kind of social relationship, that the boys played together? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Jewish boys played together, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish boys, but what about the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the introduction of religion, it ended. I had some--

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did you do on a recess, when you had a break?

speaker: interviewee
text: The usual-- played ball.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: With boys. And we used to go to the park. And the normal, [INAUDIBLE] to wander through the city, which was very interesting because very beautiful city with many things to offer. And that's all I can tell you. It was nothing unusual.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever visit the homes of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until religion was introduced.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were about eight then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, no, because in the introduction of religion, was immediately segregated the children and introduced barriers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Cheder too, or to-- did you have a Hebrew education, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father tried very hard to arrange for me a Hebrew religious education. But I did not respond to it, and finally my father-- being a very intelligent man-- realized that by forcing me, he only increased the resentment. The result was that I had no religious education. I was free. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. No question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who trained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was trained by a very pleasant young teacher. And I could sing my [INAUDIBLE]. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's somewhere at the end of February-- I couldn't tell you it. I don't remember any longer, but I used-- I read the [INAUDIBLE], and I also read from the Torah, which I still am able, occasionally, to do. And then I-- there was a reception in my house, and I delivered a discourse on [INAUDIBLE]. Namely, there are, as you know, there are [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to change our tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by all means.
